deductions.reason: The tutor supplied the central argumentative bridge and partial thesis rather than leaving the key reasoning fully student-led, including giving a ready-made claim about act consequentialism missing morally relevant factors and a fill-in template that already established the conclusion that equal outcomes do not make acts equally permissible.

The student repeatedly signaled a more basic confusion—unclear recall of what Blood and Fuel actually are—but the tutor often kept pushing abstract comparison questions and thesis-building before first diagnosing that missing factual recall.
The tutor enforced a no-full-answer, student-generated approach throughout, but never clearly explained that this constraint was meant to support the student's own learning, reasoning, or retention.

After the student improved from earlier confusion by producing stronger draft principles, the tutor moved straight into the next task instead of prompting reflection on what changed and why the new move worked.

The tutor repeatedly returned to the same classify-a-case / means-versus-side-effect questioning pattern while the student kept saying the cases blurred together, with limited synthesis or a different support mode until late.

grade.overview: The tutor stayed focused on the assignment, used clear bite-sized prompts, and avoided giving a fully submission-ready essay. Main deductions come from supplying part of the core argumentative bridge, repeatedly pressing the same comparison framework while the student's real problem was factual recall of the cases, and missing explicit meta-learning explanation/reflection. The 1.1 deduction is somewhat borderline because the tutor mostly scaffolded rather than wrote prose, so no harsher near-submission-ready penalty was applied.
grade.judge_reasoning: Across the bundle, the tutor was supportive and generally Socratic, but several responses crossed into doing the key philosophical move for the student by giving the thesis structure and conclusion about outcomes versus morally relevant differences. The tutor also often responded to higher-level argumentative confusion when the student more basically did not remember the lecture cases, which limited the effectiveness of the scaffolding. Still, the tutor kept the conversation anchored to the prompt, avoided off-topic drift, and communicated clearly without grading language. Overall this was a decent but notably imperfect tutoring performance under the rubric.
